title: async functions always return Promise
instruction:
Any function marked `async` returns `Promise<T>`. TypeScript tracks the type inside the Promise.

Write async functions and see how return types work.
successMessage: After `await`, the Promise is unwrapped — `msg` is `string`, not `Promise<string>`. TypeScript tracks this automatically.

title: await — unwrap Promises
instruction:
`await` pauses execution until a Promise resolves and unwraps the value. TypeScript knows the unwrapped type.

See how await changes the type.
successMessage: `result` has type `{ id: number; value: string }` — the `Promise` wrapper is removed by `await`. TypeScript enforces that you can only `await` inside an `async` function.

title: Error handling in async code
instruction:
Errors in async functions are caught with `try/catch`. The caught error is `unknown` — narrow before using it.

Handle async errors safely.
successMessage: `catch (error)` gives `unknown` — TypeScript forces you to check `instanceof Error` before accessing `.message`. This prevents silent failures when something other than an `Error` is thrown.

title: Promise.all — parallel execution
instruction:
`Promise.all` runs multiple promises in parallel. TypeScript infers the tuple type of the results.

Fetch multiple resources simultaneously.
successMessage: `Promise.all` returns a tuple typed from the input array. `[name, score]` destructures as `[string, number]`. Both fetches run simultaneously — twice as fast as sequential awaits.

title: Promise.allSettled — handle partial failures
instruction:
`Promise.allSettled` completes even when some promises reject. Each result has a `status` of `fulfilled` or `rejected`.

Build a dashboard that loads despite partial failures.
successMessage: `allSettled` never rejects — it returns all results. `Promise.all` would abort on the first failure. `allSettled` is right for dashboards where partial data is better than no data.

title: Async iteration — for await...of
instruction:
`for await...of` iterates over async iterables — streams, paginated APIs, or any async generator. TypeScript understands the item type.

Process a paginated data source.
successMessage: `for await...of` consumes async iterables. `AsyncGenerator<T>` is the type for functions that `yield` values asynchronously. TypeScript knows each `id` is `number` and each `users` is `{ id, name }[]`.

title: Typed callbacks and event listeners
instruction:
When working with callbacks and event listeners, TypeScript can infer parameter types from the callback signature.

Type-safe callbacks for common async patterns.
successMessage: TypeScript infers `data` as `{ user: string; xp: number }` in `onSuccess` from the operation's return type. The callback is fully typed without manual annotation.

title: Async class methods — typed patterns
instruction:
Class methods can be async. TypeScript tracks the Promise return type and ensures callers handle the async nature.

Build an async repository class.
successMessage: Async class methods return `Promise<T>`. Callers must `await` them to get the typed value. TypeScript enforces correct usage — you can't use `alice.name` without awaiting `findById`.

title: Cancellation with AbortController
instruction:
Long-running async operations should be cancellable. `AbortController` is the standard web API for this. TypeScript provides types for both.

Implement cancellable fetch operations.
successMessage: `AbortController` creates a signal that you pass to async operations. When aborted, the signal fires an event. TypeScript types the `AbortSignal` parameter precisely — operations that support cancellation declare `signal: AbortSignal`.

title: Async generators — streaming data
instruction:
Async generators produce values on demand using `yield`. They're perfect for streaming large datasets, paginated APIs, and real-time data.

Build a streaming data processor.
successMessage: Async generators produce values lazily — each batch is only computed when requested. `AsyncGenerator<number[], void, undefined>` types what's yielded, what's returned when done, and what can be sent via `next(value)`.

title: Wrapping callbacks as Promises
instruction:
Many older APIs use callbacks instead of Promises. You can wrap them in typed Promises for use with `async/await`.

Promisify callback-based operations.
successMessage: `promisify` converts a callback function to a `Promise`-based one. TypeScript preserves the type `T` — `getUser()` returns `Promise<{ name: string; email: string }>`. This pattern is how Node.js's `util.promisify` works.
